title: Multi-model adversarial review in IMPLEMENT verification fan-out
description:
The IMPLEMENT phase's adversarial verification fans out to 5 parallel reviewers (`code-reviewer`, `security-reviewer`, `ux-reviewer`, `technical-writer`, `verifier`) — all Claude (Sonnet/Haiku) subagents dispatched via the `Agent` tool. A single model family reviewing model-generated code shares blind spots, and bogus/hallucinated findings are never cross-checked against an independent model.

The core insight from the source article (and the Milvus piece it adapts) is that **the more *different* models you throw at a PR review, the lower the hallucination/false-positive rate** — real bugs get corroborated across models, while one-off hallucinations do not.

## Current Behavior

- `skills/team/SKILL.md` dispatches the 5 reviewers, all Claude subagents.
- `code-reviewer` finds issues; `code-review/SKILL.md` aggregates verdicts with severity (CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW) and hard/soft gates.
- No reviewer runs on a non-Claude model, so cross-model corroboration is impossible.

## Desired Behavior

- Add an opt-in capability to fan the bug-finding review out across **multiple distinct models/tools** — e.g. a Claude subagent plus Codex CLI, Cursor Bugbot, and/or Gemini CLI.
- An aggregator reconciles their findings into one ranked report: dedupe overlapping findings, tag each with "corroborated by N models," and flag single-model-only findings for extra scrutiny before they gate.
- Gate semantics from `code-review/SKILL.md` are preserved.

## Implementation Considerations

1. External reviewers invoked via `Bash` (e.g. `codex`, `cursor`); detect availability and **degrade gracefully** (skip, don't fail) when a tool isn't installed/authenticated.
2. Reconciliation lives either in `code-reviewer` or a new aggregator step; corroboration count becomes an input to the verdict.
3. Keep it opt-in / configurable — single-model review stays the default for users without external CLIs.
4. Watch token/cost and latency; the parallel fan-out should remain parallel.

## Acceptance Criteria

- The review step can dispatch ≥2 distinct models when configured.
- Findings corroborated by multiple models are marked as such; single-model findings are demoted or flagged for extra validation.
- With no external tools present, behavior is identical to today (graceful degradation).

Source: "Using AI to write better code more slowly" — Nolan Lawson (https://nolanlawson.com/2026/05/25/using-ai-to-write-better-code-more-slowly/)
notes:
gstack reference implementation: its `/codex` skill runs an independent OpenAI Codex review alongside Claude (it also has a Gemini E2E path) — a proven instance of this bead's "≥2 distinct models" requirement. Patterns worth lifting:

- Invocation + graceful degradation: external model called via a Bash CLI; gstack detects availability and *skips* when the tool is absent/unauthenticated (matches AC #3 — behavior identical to today with no external tools present).
- Uniform contract: hold the external reviewer to the same fresh-context, Conventional-Comments output format team's reviewers already use, so the aggregator can reconcile findings without special-casing.
- Sovereignty framing (gstack's "User Sovereignty" principle): when models *agree*, treat it as strong signal but still surface to the human rather than auto-acting — useful for mapping corroboration-count → verdict here, and dovetails with team-czy's escape-hatch design.

No new idea vs this bead — gstack is a working precedent, especially for the graceful-degradation detection and the uniform output contract the aggregator depends on.

title: Enforce zero-tolerance quality gate across all pipeline phases
description: Every phase of the TEAM pipeline must catch and resolve quality issues before advancing. No code ships with known quality defects — lint violations, type errors, test failures, review findings, or security issues. The pipeline should fail loudly and block progression when any quality check does not pass clean.
close_reason: Implemented zero-tolerance quality gates: promoted code-reviewer to hard gate, elevated HIGH security findings to blocking, made verifier fail when no checks exist, made post-write-validate block on structural errors, added REVISE-plan warning at human gate

title: Auto-revise plan on critical/major critique findings until all reviewers pass
description:
Currently the plan critique gate is a single pass: plan-critic reviews, then the user approves or rejects. If the critic finds critical or major issues, the user sees them but the plan is not automatically revised — the user must manually reject and request changes.

## Desired Behavior

When plan.critiqued is recorded with critical (C) or major (M) findings:
1. The router should automatically emit plan.revision-requested with the critique findings as feedback
2. The planner should consume plan.revision-requested, read the critique, and produce a revised plan.drafted
3. The plan-critic should re-review the revised plan
4. This cycle repeats until the critic returns a verdict with zero critical and zero major findings
5. Only then does the plan proceed to the human gate for approval

The human gate remains — the user still approves the final plan. But they should never see a plan that has unresolved critical/major issues from the critic.

## Current Flow
```
plan.drafted → plan-critic → plan.critiqued → [human gate] → plan.approved
```

## Target Flow
```
plan.drafted → plan-critic → plan.critiqued
  ├─ if critical > 0 OR major > 0:
  │    → plan.revision-requested (auto, with critique feedback)
  │    → planner consumes, produces revised plan.drafted
  │    → plan-critic re-reviews (loop back)
  ├─ if critical == 0 AND major == 0:
  │    → [human gate] → plan.approved
  └─ safety valve: max 3 revision cycles, then escalate to user
```

## Implementation Considerations

1. The planner already consumes plan.revision-requested (see registry.json). The wiring exists — the router just needs to auto-emit it instead of waiting for the user.
2. Need a cycle counter to prevent infinite revision loops. After 3 cycles with unresolved critical/major, escalate to the user with all accumulated critique history.
3. The plan artifact path should be stable across revisions (overwrite, not create new files) so the critic always reads the latest version.
4. Each revision cycle should append events to the log: plan.revision-requested → plan.drafted → plan.critiqued. The seq numbers will track the revision count.
5. The router skill (skills/team/SKILL.md) gate handling section needs updating. Currently the human gate fires immediately after plan.critiqued. It should first check the critique verdict.
6. Minor findings (m) should NOT trigger auto-revision — they are informational and can be addressed during implementation.
close_reason: Auto-revision pre-pass implemented in skills/team/SKILL.md. Any non-PASS critic verdict triggers up to 3 auto-revision rounds before human gate.

title: Build agent behavioral evals to detect regression across model changes
description:
Design and implement evals that test TEAM pipeline agents for behavioral correctness — not static linting, but actual agent execution against synthetic inputs with scored outputs. The goal is to detect regression when underlying LLM models change (e.g., new Sonnet/Opus releases).

References:
- https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents
- https://github.com/langchain-ai/agentevals

Key questions to answer:
- What eval framework to use (agentevals, custom, braintrust, etc.)
- Which agents are highest-priority to eval (planner, implementer, reviewers)
- What synthetic inputs to use (feature requests, bug reports, code samples)
- How to score outputs (rubrics, LLM-as-judge, trajectory analysis)
- How to integrate into CI or pre-release workflow
- How to handle non-determinism in agent outputs
notes:
gstack reference implementation (Garry Tan's plugin) already answers this bead's open questions with a working harness — port the architecture rather than picking a third-party framework:

- Framework: a custom harness on the repo's test runner (gstack uses Bun test), not agentevals/braintrust — lighter, no extra dep.
- Tiered pyramid: (1) static validation — free, <5s, runs every PR as the gate tier; (2) E2E — invoke the agent/pipeline via a `claude -p` subprocess runner and assert on real output; (3) LLM-as-judge — a Sonnet judge scores E2E output against rubrics (clarity, completeness).
- Non-determinism / CI: split a deterministic *gate tier* (blocks PRs) from a *periodic tier* (non-deterministic + expensive model runs, on a weekly cron). Answers both "integrate into CI" and "handle non-determinism."
- Cost control: diff-based test selection — each test declares its file deps; only evals touching changed files run locally; an ALL=1 flag forces the full suite. Plus partial-result persistence (incremental JSON survives a killed run) and timestamped result files with compare/summary tooling.
- Priority agents: eval the judgment-heavy ones first (planner, implementer, reviewers) — matches this bead's instinct.

Net: a custom subprocess harness + LLM-judge + diff-selection is sufficient; no third-party eval framework needed.

title: AGENTS.md: table of contents, not encyclopedia
description:
AGENTS.md should be a compact router (~150 lines) that points agents to references, not a content repository itself. Rules:
- Keep AGENTS.md under ~150 lines
- It should function as a table of contents / router
- Each entry should point to where information lives (other files, docs, conventions)
- Do not embed content, rules, or detailed instructions directly in AGENTS.md
- If content needs to exist, put it in a referenced file and link from AGENTS.md
close_reason: Added router principle to AGENTS.md, extracted shell conventions to docs/shell-conventions.md. AGENTS.md is now 77 lines.

title: Opt-in surfacing of pre-existing bugs in touched files
description:
The source article observes that AI review often surfaces *pre-existing* bugs, and that chasing them — writing tests, fixing latent flaws — improves codebase health even though it doesn't raise velocity: *"the happy-path of a complex architecture is less interesting than its failure modes."* TEAM's reviewers are scoped strictly to the diff, so latent bugs in touched files are invisible by design.

## Current Behavior

- `agents/code-reviewer.md` explicitly states: *"Do NOT review files outside the diff unless they are directly affected by the changes."*
- Pre-existing bugs in files the PR touches are out of scope. The pipeline optimizes for diff correctness and velocity, never codebase health.

## Desired Behavior

- An **opt-in** mode where reviewers may flag latent/pre-existing bugs in files the PR touches.
- Such findings are reported as **separate, non-blocking** items (they must not block the current PR) and can optionally be spun off into new beads via `bd create --deps discovered-from:<id>`, so they're captured rather than lost.
- Reframes review from a diff-only gate into an optional codebase-health pass.

## Implementation Considerations

1. Add a flag/setting that relaxes the diff-only rule in `agents/code-reviewer.md`; default stays diff-only.
2. Pre-existing findings are **non-blocking by default** and clearly separated from diff findings in the report.
3. Optionally auto-create `discovered-from` beads for each, linking back to the current topic id.
4. Document the trade-off: extra tokens, scope-creep risk, and the velocity-vs-health framing from the article.

## Acceptance Criteria

- In opt-in mode, a planted pre-existing bug in a touched file is reported as non-blocking and (optionally) creates a `discovered-from` bead.
- Default mode is unchanged (diff-only, no pre-existing-bug reporting).

Source: "Using AI to write better code more slowly" — Nolan Lawson (https://nolanlawson.com/2026/05/25/using-ai-to-write-better-code-more-slowly/)

title: Pre-PR comprehension gate: explainer + failure-mode walkthrough (grill-me)
description:
The source article's central practice: *"Ask an agent how your PR works and how it might fail. Have it write Markdown docs with Mermaid charts if necessary. Use Matt Pocock's /grill-me skill until you understand the entire PR front-to-back."* TEAM's human gates land *before* code (Design, Structure); nothing ensures the human understands the *finished* diff before it ships.

## Current Behavior

- Human gates are at DESIGN and STRUCTURE — both before implementation.
- After verification, the PR phase updates the changelog, commits, and opens the PR. There is no checkpoint where the human is brought to a confident understanding of the actual diff and its failure modes.

## Desired Behavior

An **optional** comprehension step before/within the PR phase that:

1. Generates a "How it works / How it might fail" document for the diff, including a **Mermaid diagram** of the changed control/data flow and an explicit enumeration of failure modes.
2. Optionally provides an interactive `/team-grill` that quizzes the human on the PR (or lets the human quiz the agent) until they understand it front-to-back.

## Implementation Considerations

1. Mirror the `/eng-design-doc-review` pattern: a self-contained skill that dispatches the built-in `general-purpose` subagent (no custom agent), keeping the "thin harness, fat skills" model.
2. Mermaid is already available in the plugin; output lands in `docs/plans/<id>/`.
3. **Optional and off the QRSPI hard path** — like `/eng-design-doc-review`, it presents output and never blocks or mutates pipeline state.
4. Reuse `AskUserQuestion` for the interactive grill loop.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Running the step produces a failure-mode doc + Mermaid diagram of the diff in `docs/plans/<id>/`.
- The step is optional and absent from the QRSPI phase table; skipping it leaves today's flow unchanged.

Source: "Using AI to write better code more slowly" — Nolan Lawson (https://nolanlawson.com/2026/05/25/using-ai-to-write-better-code-more-slowly/)

title: Project-configurable reviewer quality bar (custom "bug" definition)
description:
The source article notes: *"You can add your own definition of 'bug' if you want — mine has stipulations about the KISS and DRY principles, writing accessible HTML/JSX, using proper indexes for SQL queries, etc."* TEAM's reviewer quality bar is fixed in the plugin; a consuming repo can't declare its own review criteria.

## Current Behavior

- `code-reviewer` loads a fixed set of methodology skills: `code-review`, `solid-principles`, `engineering-standards`.
- The definition of "what counts as a bug / quality issue" is baked into the plugin. A consuming project has no first-class way to add project-specific rules (KISS/DRY enforcement, accessible HTML/JSX, SQL index usage, perf budgets, naming conventions).

## Desired Behavior

- A consuming repo can declare project-specific review criteria that `code-reviewer` loads **in addition to** the built-in standards.
- Findings cite which project rule they violate, just as built-in findings cite the engineering-standards checklist item.

## Implementation Considerations

1. Follow the existing plugin-settings pattern (`.claude/team.local.md` with YAML frontmatter + markdown body) so config is per-project and version-controlled.
2. `code-reviewer` reads the file if present and appends its rules to the review brief; absence of the file = today's behavior.
3. Document the convention in `README.md` / `docs/architecture.md` with a worked example (e.g. a KISS/DRY + a11y + SQL-index rule set).
4. Consider whether `security-reviewer` and `ux-reviewer` should read the same file or their own scoped sections.

## Acceptance Criteria

- A project can add a custom rule and see it enforced in a review, with a citation back to the rule.
- With no config file present, review behavior is unchanged.

Source: "Using AI to write better code more slowly" — Nolan Lawson (https://nolanlawson.com/2026/05/25/using-ai-to-write-better-code-more-slowly/)

title: Add "abandon approach" escape hatch + worth-the-squeeze triage to verification loop
description:
The source article describes a triage workflow the current verification loop doesn't model: *fix all criticals/highs; **skip** highs/mediums where the juice isn't worth the squeeze; **abandon** the PR if it has so many criticals you realize the whole approach is misguided.*

## Current Behavior

- IMPLEMENT runs a typed failure-class retry loop (max 5 rounds). It keeps fixing and re-verifying.
- Severity exists (CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW per `code-review/SKILL.md`), but the loop treats failures uniformly as retry fuel — there is no first-class "this approach is fundamentally wrong, stop and escalate" outcome, and no explicit "defer this low-value finding" path. A doomed approach burns all 5 rounds before surfacing to the human.

## Desired Behavior

1. **Abandon/rethink verdict.** When critical findings exceed a threshold or the same criticals recur across rounds, pause the loop and ask the human (via `AskUserQuestion`) whether to abandon the slice/approach — rather than silently exhausting the retry budget.
2. **Worth-the-squeeze skip.** Allow the orchestrator/human to mark MEDIUM/LOW findings as "won't fix — not worth the squeeze" with a recorded rationale, so they neither block nor get silently dropped.

## Implementation Considerations

1. Extend the retry-loop logic in `skills/team/SKILL.md` / `skills/qrspi-workflow/SKILL.md`.
2. Add the abandon/rethink branch as an `AskUserQuestion` option (consistent with existing human-gate verdicts).
3. Record skipped findings + rationale in the pipeline artifact so the decision is auditable.
4. Define the "too many criticals" threshold (count, or recurrence across N rounds) — make it tunable.

## Acceptance Criteria

- A PR with N+ unresolved/recurring criticals triggers an explicit abandon/rethink prompt instead of more silent retries.
- Low-value findings can be marked won't-fix with a logged rationale and stop blocking the gate.

Source: "Using AI to write better code more slowly" — Nolan Lawson (https://nolanlawson.com/2026/05/25/using-ai-to-write-better-code-more-slowly/)

title: Review and improve skill invocation/composition (entry point vs building block)
description:
# SKILL AUTHORING: entry point vs building block

  Every skill you write lives on one or both of two invocation surfaces. Decide which
  BEFORE writing it, because the wiring is completely different. The core rule that makes
  this work: **skills are documents, not functions.** A skill is never "called" by another
  skill through a skill-invocation tool — composition means another skill *reads this one's
  file* or *spawns a subagent* to run it. The skill-invocation tool is reserved for the
  top-level surface (user types it, or the model auto-invokes it).

  ## Step 1 — Classify this skill

  | Question | If yes → |
  |----------|----------|
  | Will a USER trigger this by intent ("ship it", "review this", "I have an idea")? | It's an **ENTRY POINT** → wire Surface 1 |
  | Will ANOTHER skill pull this in as a step of a larger workflow? | It's a **BUILDING BLOCK** → wire Surface 2 |
  | Both? (common — e.g. an ideation skill a user invokes AND a planning skill inlines) | Wire **both** surfaces |

  Classification is not a flag you set; it's a consequence of how the skill is reached.
  Your job is to wire the path(s) you intend and not leak onto the one you don't.

  ## Surface 1 — Make it an ENTRY POINT (user / model-invoked)

  Two signals, both convention. There is usually NO hard flag — auto-invocation is driven
  by text the model reads:

  1. **Write the description as a router.** Lead with WHAT it does, then add explicit
     trigger language naming the user intents that should fire it:
     ```yaml
     description: |
       <one line: what this does>.
       Proactively invoke this skill (do NOT answer directly) when the user
       <intent A>, <intent B>, or says "<phrase>", "<phrase>".
     ```
     The model auto-invokes by matching this text. Specific intents + example phrases =
     reliable triggering. Vague descriptions = missed or wrong triggers.

  2. **Add it to the routing map** (the project's CLAUDE.md / agent instructions), one line:
     `- <user intent> → invoke /<skill>`. This map is GUIDANCE the agent reads, not a
     code gate — keep it in sync with the description.

  3. **To make it user-explicit-ONLY (no auto-invoke):** omit the proactive-invoke
     language AND leave it off the routing map. If your host supports a hard opt-out flag
     (e.g. a `disable-model-invocation` / `sensitive` frontmatter field), set it — but know
     that on hosts that don't honor it, the description is the only control. Use this for
     destructive/irreversible skills (deploy, freeze, force-push) so they require an
     explicit ask.

  ## Surface 2 — Make it a BUILDING BLOCK (composed by another skill)

  You don't "declare" composability — any skill file can be composed. What you decide is
  HOW a parent pulls it in. Pick by whether the parent needs coordination or isolation:

  **(a) INLINE — parent reads this skill's file and follows it.**
  Use when the parent runs children sequentially and weaves their outputs into one result.
  The parent instruction looks like:
  > "Follow <child>/SKILL.md — all sections, full depth. Skip sections: <list>."
  Authoring implications for the CHILD:
  - Keep sections independently runnable and clearly headed (parents skip by header).
  - Don't assume you own the whole conversation; a parent may run you mid-flow.

  **(b) SUBAGENT — parent spawns a fresh-context agent to run this.**
  Use when the parent needs an unbiased perspective (adversarial review), or parallelism
  (N variants/specialists at once). The parent instruction looks like:
  > "Dispatch this as a subagent (Agent/Task tool, fresh context). Launch all N in a
  >  single message for parallelism. Return only the conclusion."
  Authoring implications for the CHILD:
  - Make it self-contained: it gets a clean window, so state what to read up front.
  - Return a conclusion, not a transcript (protects the parent's context budget).

  **(d) PREREQUISITE OFFER — parent offers this when input is missing.**
  Use for "you need X before I can run." The parent asks via a user question:
  > "No <artifact> found. Options: A) run /<child> now  B) skip and proceed."
  If accepted, the parent then inlines the child (mechanism a).

  ## Hard invariants (do not violate)

  1. **Never compose via the skill-invocation tool.** A skill must not call another skill
     through the Skill/slash tool. Composition = Read-and-follow (inline) OR spawn-subagent.
     The invocation tool is the user/model entry-point surface only.
  2. **Heavy or adversarial sub-work goes to a subagent**, so the parent's window stays lean
     and the sub-work stays unbiased. Sequential/coordinated sub-work goes inline.
  3. **A skill can serve both surfaces** — that's fine and common. Just ensure its
     description triggers correctly AND its sections survive being inlined/subagented.
  4. **Don't auto-trigger irreversible skills.** Strip proactive-invoke language and (if
     supported) set the host opt-out flag; require the user to ask.
  5. **Keep description ⇔ routing map in sync.** They are two views of the same triggering
     contract; drift causes mis-routing.

  ## Acceptance checklist
  - [ ] Classified: entry point, building block, or both — and wired only the intended path(s).
  - [ ] Entry point: description has WHAT + explicit trigger intents/phrases; added to routing map.
  - [ ] Irreversible skill: proactive language removed; host opt-out flag set if available.
  - [ ] Building block: chose inline (sequential/coordinated) vs subagent (isolated/parallel) deliberately.
  - [ ] If subagented: skill is self-contained and returns a conclusion, not a transcript.
  - [ ] If inlined: sections are headed and independently runnable.
  - [ ] No skill invokes another via the skill-invocation tool.

title: Improve skill context management: add CONTEXT DISCIPLINE block (payload vs working set)
description:
Improve how the Team plugin's skills manage the context window. The block below
is the **exact prompt text to be used** — embed it into the skills as-is. Where
it lives (a shared composable methodology skill loaded by skills, vs. inlined
into each fat skill) is a design decision for implementation; the wording is
not. Part of the fat-skills rebuild (related: team-wuz, team-5ww).

---

# CONTEXT DISCIPLINE (how this skill manages the context window)

## The principle: two token economies, treated oppositely

There are two kinds of tokens, and they have opposite cost curves. Optimize them
differently:

1. **The payload** — these instructions, your system prompt, the skill text. It is
   prompt-cached and amortized: paid roughly once, cheap to repeat. Do NOT compress
   it for size's sake. Completeness here is cheap. A long, complete instruction beats
   a terse, ambiguous one.
2. **The working set** — everything you READ and GENERATE while executing: file
   contents, command output, sub-results, your own intermediate reasoning dumped
   into the window. This is uncached and grows without bound. THIS is what you ration.

Rule of thumb: **be generous with the payload, ruthless with the working set.**
Never "summarize to save space" — prefer to never pull the bytes into the window
in the first place.

## One-time setup

Pick a durable artifacts directory for this project and use it for all handoffs and
checkpoints. Default: `.agent/<task-slug>/` at the repo root (create if missing).
Call this `<ARTIFACTS>` below. Everything you'd otherwise "remember in the chat"
goes here as a file instead.

## Execution rules (in priority order)

### 1. Offload state to disk — don't carry it in the conversation
- Write structured artifacts (decisions, the plan, findings, the doc under review)
  to `<ARTIFACTS>/*.md`. Read them back when needed; don't keep them resident.
- When a multi-step task pauses or risks compaction, checkpoint to
  `<ARTIFACTS>/checkpoint-<timestamp>.md`: branch, what's done, decisions made,
  remaining work, open questions. A fresh window resumes from the file, not from
  replayed history. Checkpoints are append-only — never overwrite.
- Skills hand off to each other through these files, not through context. The
  producer writes `<ARTIFACTS>/design-<branch>.md`; the consumer globs newest-first
  and reads it once.

### 2. Delegate heavy reading to subagents — keep only the conclusion
- If a step requires sweeping many files, comparing variants, or any broad
  fan-out, spawn a subagent (Task/Agent tool) to do it. The subagent burns ITS
  window on the file dumps; you receive only its final answer.
- Prefer read-only/search subagents for "find/where/which" questions. Launch
  independent subagents in parallel (one message, multiple calls) when their work
  doesn't depend on each other.
- Once you delegate a search, do not also run it yourself — wait for the result.

### 3. Search, don't read whole files
- For "where/what/which" questions, use semantic search if available, else targeted
  grep/glob. Pull excerpts and specific line ranges — never `cat` a large file to
  "see what's there."
- Read only the section you need. When you know the symbol/section, read that
  offset, not the whole file.
- Don't re-read a file you just edited to confirm the edit; trust the tool result.

### 4. Don't copy when you can reference
- When constructing inputs for a sub-task or test, extract the ~dozens of relevant
  lines, never paste a 1000+ line file. Large irrelevant context causes timeouts and
  5-10x slowdowns, not just cost.

## Authoring rules (when writing/editing this skill)
- Keep the payload focused on THIS skill's job; don't fold in unrelated capability.
- Load advanced/optional instruction blocks only when the skill actually needs them.
- Size is a feature-bloat smell, not a hard limit — if the skill grew, ask "what
  capability was added?" before compressing. Cut prose only as a last resort.

## Before each action, gate yourself
- **Before reading:** "Do I need the whole file, or a section? Can a search answer
  this without a read? Should a subagent read it instead of me?"
- **Before spawning subagents:** "Is this broad enough to be worth delegating? Can
  these run in parallel?"
- **Before continuing a long task:** "Is there state here I'd lose on compaction?
  Checkpoint it to `<ARTIFACTS>` now."

## Anti-patterns (do not do these)
- Reading entire files to 'get oriented'.
- Keeping a design doc, plan, or prior findings resident in chat across many turns
  instead of writing them to `<ARTIFACTS>` and re-reading on demand.
- Pasting large files into sub-task prompts or test fixtures.
- Doing a broad multi-file sweep inline when a subagent could return just the answer.
- Compressing your own instructions to "save tokens" — that's the cached payload;
  it's not where the cost is.

title: Enforce red-green-refactor in IMPLEMENT phase via fat skills (re-implement PR #10, no custom agents)
description:
Re-implement the red-green-refactor (R-G-R) IMPLEMENT-phase enforcement from
GitHub PR #10 (`feat: enforce red-green-refactor with role-separated agents`,
branch `2026-05-22-red-green-refactor-tdd`) — but in the **fat-skills**
architecture, NOT the agent-heavy form the PR shipped.

## Why this is a re-implementation, not a merge

PR #10 delivers the right *behavior* but the wrong *shape* for the new world.
It adds three custom agents (`red-author`, `green-author`, `refactor-author`),
deletes `test-architect`, and grows the inventory 13 → 15. That is the opposite
direction from the **"thin harness, fat skills"** model now in use (see
CHANGELOG / `skills/eng-design-doc-review/SKILL.md`), where a skill is
self-contained — it carries the full brief inline and dispatches the built-in
`general-purpose` subagent for context isolation, rather than introducing a new
named agent per role.

This bead supersedes PR #10. Keep the PR's design as prior art; discard its
agent surgery.

## Behavior to deliver (preserve from PR #10)

Per **vertical slice**, run a red → green → refactor loop, then a final
aggregate review gate:

1. **Red** (per slice, not once up-front): write the slice's failing acceptance
   tests, scoped to that slice only. Commit `test: <slice>`.
   - **Red gate** (mechanical): tests must fail with assertion errors, not
     crashes.
2. **Green** (per slice): smallest change that makes the slice's red tests pass.
   Commit `feat: <slice>`. Scope-fenced against refactoring, opportunistic
   abstraction, and editing the acceptance-test script.
   - **Green gate** (mechanical): 3-attempt cap per slice; prior slices must
     still pass (regression check).
3. **Refactor** (per slice, optional): runs only on green; re-runs the full
   suite after each structural change; produces an optional `refactor: <slice>`
   commit (no commit on a no-op). If it cannot leave the suite green, it reverts
   and reports a no-op.
4. Advance to the next slice.
5. After all slices: the existing **5-reviewer aggregate gate** (5-round retry).
   `implementer` is retained ONLY for the typed-failure-class review-fix loop
   driven by that gate.

## Fat-skills constraint (the reframe)

- **No new custom agents.** The red / green / refactor role separation is
  achieved by three distinct inline briefs in the IMPLEMENT skill(s)
  (`skills/team-implement/`, `skills/team/`), each dispatched as a separate
  `general-purpose` subagent call per slice. Fresh subagent context per role
  provides the same isolation PR #10 got from separate agent files.
- Do not recreate `red-author.md` / `green-author.md` / `refactor-author.md`.
- Revisit whether `test-architect` should be deleted at all, or simply absorbed
  into the red brief — decide during design, not up front.
- Keep `skills/team/registry.json` and the registry-sync hook honest for
  whatever agent set actually remains.

## Build discipline: red-green-refactor (TDD)

Implement THIS bead the same way the feature enforces:

- **Red first:** write failing acceptance tests asserting the fat-skills R-G-R
  behavior (skill content, inline briefs, gate semantics, commit-message
  conventions). PR #10's `tests/acceptance/red-green-refactor-tdd.sh` (42
  checks) is a starting template, but its assertions target the *agent-file*
  shape and must be rewritten for the fat-skills shape (no `red-author.md` etc.).
- **Green:** smallest skill edits to pass.
- **Refactor:** tighten while staying green.

## Prior art / source material

- GitHub PR #10 body + branch `2026-05-22-red-green-refactor-tdd`.
- QRSPI artifacts on that branch:
  `docs/plans/2026-05-22-red-green-refactor-tdd/{task,questions,research,design,structure,plan}.md`
  (design + structure were both human-approved 2026-05-22; 7 numbered design
  decisions, 3 vertical slices).
- Acceptance harness: `tests/acceptance/red-green-refactor-tdd.sh`.
- Related: closed research bead team-3gk (thin entry points vs self-contained
  skills); bead team-5ww (differentiate invoked vs composable skills) is part of
  the same skill-architecture rethink.

## Done when

- Per-slice R-G-R enforcement (red gate, green gate w/ 3-attempt cap +
  prior-slice regression check, refactor self-verifies green) is enforced
  through fat skills with NO new custom agents.
- The `test:` / `feat:` / `refactor:` per-slice commit conventions hold.
- The 5-reviewer aggregate gate and the narrowed `implementer` review-fix loop
  are preserved.
- Acceptance tests for the fat-skills shape pass; registry-sync hook is green.
- Docs (`README.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `docs/architecture.md`, `docs/index.md`,
  `skills/qrspi-workflow/SKILL.md`) describe the fat-skills R-G-R flow.

title: Move pipeline artifacts from docs/plans/ to git-ignored .team/
description:
## Problem

Pipeline artifacts (`task.md`, `questions.md`, `research.md`, `design.md`,
`structure.md`, `plan.md`, etc.) are written under `docs/plans/<id>/`, which
lives **inside the repo and gets committed to git**. These artifacts are
transient *working state*, not deliverables — for end users running `/team`,
they clutter the working tree and pollute git history with pipeline scratch
data.

State should be local-only: present on disk for compaction defense and
session recovery, but never tracked.

## Proposal

Move the artifact root from `docs/plans/<id>/` to **`.team/<id>/`**, and ensure
`.team/` is git-ignored so artifacts never enter version control.

`.gitignore` in this repo **already reserves `.team/`** (line 8,
`# Team pipeline state`) — the ignore target exists; only the migration is
missing.

## Scope (grounded in the current tree)

- **~285 references to `docs/plans/` across ~50 files** must be updated:
  - every `skills/*/SKILL.md` (entry points + methodology skills)
  - the agent files under `agents/`
  - the two runtime hooks that scan the artifact dir for active topics:
    `hooks/session-start-recover.mjs` and `hooks/pre-compact-anchor.mjs`
  - docs: `README.md`, `docs/architecture.md`, `CHANGELOG.md`, `CLAUDE.md`,
    `skills/team/registry.json`
- **32 artifact files are currently tracked** under `docs/plans/` (this
  project's own dogfooding history). A migration decision is needed (see open
  questions).
- For **end-user repos**, the plugin can't touch a user's global
  `core.excludesfile`. Decide how `.team/` gets ignored on their side
  (documented setup, auto-append to the project `.gitignore` on first run, or
  rely on the user's global excludes).

## Open questions (for design, not prescribed here)

- **Historical artifacts:** what happens to the 32 already-committed
  `docs/plans/` files? Keep them in place as a record, relocate the keepers to
  a non-ignored docs path, or drop them from tracking?
- **End-user ignore mechanism:** auto-add `.team/` to the project `.gitignore`
  on first pipeline run, document it as a one-time setup step, or both?
- **Layout:** `docs/plans/` currently mixes flat (`<id>-task.md`) and nested
  (`<id>/task.md`) artifacts. Standardize on `.team/<id>/<artifact>.md` during
  the move?

## Done when

- The pipeline writes all artifacts under `.team/<id>/`.
- `.team/` is git-ignored so artifacts never enter git (project-level here;
  end-user mechanism decided and documented).
- All `docs/plans/` references (skills, agents, hooks, docs, registry) are
  updated to `.team/`.
- The state-recovery hooks scan `.team/` instead of `docs/plans/`.
- A migration story for the existing tracked artifacts is decided and executed.

---
_Migrated from GitHub issue bostonaholic/team#15._

title: Differentiate invoked entry-point skills from composable methodology skills
description:
## Problem

The plugin ships 26 skills under `skills/`, and they fall into two conceptually
distinct kinds:

- **Invoked / entry-point skills** — meant to be *called* directly. They double
  as slash commands, carry trigger phrases and an `argument-hint` in their
  frontmatter, and form the QRSPI pipeline entry points (e.g. `team`,
  `team-design`, `team-research`, `team-structure`, `team-plan`,
  `team-worktree`, `team-implement`, `team-pr`, `team-question`, `team-fix`,
  `eng-design-doc-review`).

- **Composable / methodology skills** — *never* invoked directly. They are
  loaded by agents as reference material. Their descriptions follow the
  convention "…methodology — loaded by X agent when Y" (e.g. `solid-principles`,
  `code-review`, `systematic-debugging`, `test-first-development`, `git-commit`,
  `changelog`, `writing-prose`, `engineering-standards`,
  `refactoring-to-patterns`, `qrspi-workflow`, `technical-design-doc`,
  `documenting-decisions`, `test-driven-bug-fix`, `worktree-isolation`,
  `product-requirements-doc`).

`AGENTS.md` already states the intent — *"Entry point skills double as slash
commands. Methodology skills are loaded by agents."* — but **nothing structural
enforces or signals the difference.** The only differentiator today is a prose
convention in each skill's `description`.

## Why this matters

- **Namespace pollution / confusing UX.** Every skill auto-registers as a slash
  command. That means composable skills like `/solid-principles` or
  `/code-review` are surfaced as user-invocable commands even though invoking
  them directly does nothing useful — they're designed to be loaded *into* an
  agent's context, not run as a top-level action.
- **No machine-readable classification.** Tooling, docs, and contributors can't
  reliably tell the two kinds apart without parsing description prose.
- **Maintenance drift.** As skills are added, there's no gate that keeps the
  intended classification honest (mirrors the existing
  `registry.json` ↔ `agents/` sync invariant, which *is* enforced).

## Possible directions (to be settled in design, not prescribed here)

- A frontmatter marker (e.g. a `kind: entry-point | methodology` field) — pending
  whether Claude Code's supported skill frontmatter allows custom fields without
  breaking auto-discovery.
- A naming or directory convention (e.g. group composable skills under a
  subdirectory, or a prefix) so the split is visible at a glance.
- Investigate whether Claude Code lets a skill opt out of slash-command
  registration, so methodology skills stay loadable-by-agents but don't appear
  as invocable commands.
- A documented classification table (in `docs/architecture.md` and/or
  `AGENTS.md`) plus an optional dev hook to keep it in sync, paralleling
  `check-registry-sync.mjs`.

## Done when

- The two skill kinds are differentiated by a clear, machine-checkable mechanism
  (not just description prose).
- Composable/methodology skills no longer present as if they were directly
  invocable entry points (or it is explicitly documented why they must).
- The classification is documented and, ideally, guarded against drift.

---
_Migrated from GitHub issue bostonaholic/team#14._

title: Use Claude Code's AskUserQuestion at all interactive points in team pipeline
description: Audit found that design-author agent and several orchestrator skills describe interactive user prompts but don't reference Claude Code's built-in AskUserQuestion tool. The design-author agent prints markdown numbered lists; the team-design/team-structure human gates use free-text 'approve?' prompts; team-pr presents shipping options as text; team-implement asks worktree-vs-inplace as text. All of these are multi-choice and should use AskUserQuestion per https://code.claude.com/docs/en/tools-reference. TodoWrite usage is already consistent.
acceptance_criteria: design-author agent has AskUserQuestion in tools list and uses it for the open-questions step; team-design, team-structure, team-implement, team-pr, and team SKILL.md reference AskUserQuestion at every multi-choice user prompt; tests pass
close_reason: design-author agent and team/team-design/team-structure/team-implement/team-pr skills now route every multi-choice user prompt through Claude Code's AskUserQuestion tool. Regression locked in by tests/ask-user-question-tool-tests.sh (9 assertions). All five test suites pass. Changelog updated under Unreleased > Changed.

title: Extend topic consistency to design/structure/plan artifacts
description: team-k02 only fixed task/questions/research prompts. Extend the topic-consistency invariant to design-author, structure-planner, and planner agents — each must copy 'topic' verbatim from the predecessor artifact. Also cover team-fix's directly-written task.md. Add tests for each downstream agent.
close_reason: Extended topic-consistency invariant to design-author, structure-planner, planner, and team-fix. Tests T6-T9 now pass. 4 commits land on main locally; push pending user authorization.

title: Fix artifact frontmatter topic inconsistency
description: Pipeline agents produce inconsistent 'topic' frontmatter values across artifacts in docs/plans/<id>/. Observed: task.md has 'break-glass-kill-switch', questions.md has 'RELENG-335' (ticket id used as topic), research.md has 'RELENG-335 break-glass kill switch' (mash of id + topic, not kebab). Per qrspi-workflow.md, all artifacts in the same directory should carry the same kebab-case topic slug. Fix: clarify questioner.md, researcher.md, and qrspi-workflow.md prompts to make topic-consistency invariant explicit. Also document why ticketId only lives on task.md.
close_reason: Fixed prompt ambiguity for topic frontmatter consistency. Added tests/topic-consistency-tests.sh. Two commits land on main locally (4c03110 test, d59d59a fix); push pending user choice.

title: Teamflow: browser notifications + visual tab badge for human gates
description:
When a pipeline hits a human gate (plan approval, interview, ship confirmation), the user may be watching a different tab or not looking at the dashboard at all. There's no obvious signal that their input is needed.

## Desired Behavior

1. **Tab badge**: When a session enters a human-gate state, its tab in the Teamflow tab bar should show a visible indicator (e.g., pulsing dot, color change, or badge icon) so it stands out from tabs running autonomously.

2. **Browser notifications**: Fire a Notification API notification when a human gate activates. Message should identify which pipeline/topic needs attention (e.g., 'Pipeline "auth-refactor" is waiting for plan approval'). Respect the permission prompt — request on first gate hit, degrade gracefully if denied.

3. **Document title update**: Update `document.title` to include a prefix like '⏳' or '🔔' when any session is gate-blocked, so the browser tab itself signals attention is needed even without the dashboard being visible.

## Human Gates to Track

- Interview gate (requirements validation)
- Plan approval gate
- Ship confirmation gate
- Any future human gates added to the pipeline

## Implementation Considerations

- The SSE stream already carries gate events. The state engine in `teamflow/src/state.ts` derives gate status. The frontend just needs to react to human-gate-active states.
- Browser Notification API requires user permission. Request it lazily on first human gate, not on page load.
- Tab badge should clear when the gate resolves (plan.approved, ship.complete, etc.).
- Document title prefix should clear when no sessions have active human gates.
- Keep the notification sound/visual subtle — this is a dev tool, not an alarm.
close_reason: Teamflow UI removed — bead no longer relevant

title: Teamflow: second tab missing title, shows raw topic slug
description: When multiple sessions are open, the second tab displays the raw topic slug (e.g. 'suppress-intermediate-gate-noise') instead of a human-readable title/summary. The first tab correctly shows a title. Likely the title extraction from the event log is failing or not being applied for non-first sessions. See screenshot: the active tab reads 'suppress-intermediate-gate-noise PLAN' with no summary/title.
close_reason: Fixed: tab bar and header now show human-readable titles from data.description instead of raw kebab slugs

title: Teamflow: persist active tab across page refresh
description: When the user refreshes the teamflow dashboard, the previously active session tab should be restored. Currently a refresh likely resets to the default tab, losing the user's context. Store the active tab identifier (e.g., in localStorage or a URL hash) so it survives a full page reload.
close_reason: Closed

title: Beads watcher server: auto-dispatch TEAM pipeline on new issues
description: A long-running server process that watches the beads log/database for newly created issues and automatically dispatches the TEAM pipeline to work on them. When a new issue appears (e.g., via bd create from another session or external tooling), the server picks it up immediately and kicks off /team to implement it autonomously. This enables a fire-and-forget workflow: create issues from anywhere and they get worked on without manual intervention.
close_reason: Teamflow UI/stack removed — bead no longer relevant

title: Planner ↔ plan-critic feedback loop before human review
description: Currently the planner produces a plan that goes directly to the human gate. Instead, introduce an iterative loop: the planner emits a draft plan, the plan-critic reviews it, and if issues are found the critic's recommendations feed back to the planner for revision. This cycle repeats until the plan-critic is satisfied. Only then does the plan advance to the human reviewer (team lead) for final approval. This reduces low-quality plans reaching the human gate and catches structural/feasibility issues earlier.

title: Teamflow: show 'waiting for human' state with browser notifications
description: When the pipeline reaches a human gate (e.g., plan approval), the Teamflow dashboard should visually indicate the waiting state and optionally fire a Chrome browser notification so the user knows input is needed — even if the tab is in the background.
close_reason: Superseded by team-46l which covers the same scope with more detailed design

title: Agent status not updated when phase completes
description:
When a phase is marked COMPLETED, not all of its agents reflect the correct status:

1. **Research phase**: shows COMPLETED but file-finder still shows a blue (in-progress/pending) icon instead of a green checkmark like researcher
2. **Plan phase**: shows COMPLETED but product-owner and plan-critic still show blue icons — only planner shows the green checkmark

The phase-level status and agent-level statuses are out of sync. Either the agents that didn't run should show a skipped/not-needed state, or the dashboard should derive agent status from the phase completion event.
close_reason: Teamflow UI removed — bead no longer relevant

title: Dashboard title shows 'test-feature' instead of actual topic
description: The dashboard header displays 'Teamflow / test-feature' as the pipeline title, but the actual feature being built has a different topic. The title should be derived from the feature_requested event's topic/description, not a hardcoded or default value.
close_reason: Fixed in subsequent dashboard commits (f1ca0b2, a9adf44). Topic is correctly derived from feature.requested event data — 'test-feature' was the literal topic from a test run, not a hardcoded value.

title: Dashboard shows wrong elapsed time and timeline timestamps
description:
The Teamflow dashboard displays incorrect timing data. Two issues:

1. **Total elapsed time** (top-right corner) shows '12h 32m' when the pipeline has only been running for ~1 minute
2. **Timeline timestamps** show '4:00:00 AM' instead of the actual event time

Likely cause: the elapsed time calculation is using raw epoch/date math incorrectly, possibly treating a Unix timestamp as milliseconds or vice versa, or anchoring to midnight instead of the pipeline start time.
close_reason: Fixed in subsequent dashboard commits. Duration calculation correctly uses ms diff between startedAt and latest event timestamp. formatDuration and formatTime functions handle display correctly.

title: Serve teamflow privately at team.bostonaholic.dev
description: Set up local DNS resolution and TLS so the teamflow app can be accessed at team.bostonaholic.dev without going to the internet. Options: /etc/hosts + mkcert for .dev TLS, or fall back to .test TLD for simplicity. First version is local-only.
close_reason: Teamflow UI removed — bead no longer relevant

title: Start Teamflow server via dev server and connect pipeline sessions to it
description:
The Teamflow dashboard currently requires manual startup (node teamflow/bin/teamflow.mjs or dev demo). Instead, integrate it into the dev server command so it runs automatically alongside the development environment.

## Requirements

- Add Teamflow server startup to dev server (dev.yml or equivalent)
- The server starts once and stays running — pipeline sessions connect to it, not the other way around
- When a /team pipeline starts, it writes to .team/events.jsonl as usual; the already-running Teamflow server picks up the events via its file tailer
- No duplicate server instances — if the server is already running, pipeline sessions should just work without trying to start another one
- The dashboard should be accessible at the usual http://127.0.0.1:7425 throughout the dev session

## Current state

- teamflow/bin/teamflow.mjs starts the Fastify server with tsx
- The server tails .team/events.jsonl and broadcasts state via SSE
- Pipeline hooks (session-start-recover.mjs) do not start the server — they assume it is running or not present
- dev demo starts the server AND writes mock events (coupled concerns)

## Design considerations

- dev server should handle npm install for teamflow/ dependencies
- Consider health check to avoid starting if already running (curl http://127.0.0.1:7425/api/health)
- dev demo should detect if server is already running and skip starting its own
- Build the frontend (npm run build in teamflow/) as part of dev server setup if dist/ is stale
close_reason: Implemented: dev server command, demo dedup, build_first, acceptance tests T20-T23, doc updates

title: Multi-session tabs in Teamflow dashboard
description:
Add tabbed navigation to the Teamflow dashboard so multiple concurrent TEAM pipeline sessions can be monitored simultaneously. Each tab represents an isolated session with its own event log, phase cards, timeline, and error panel — the full UI as it exists today, scoped per session.

## Requirements

- Tab bar at the top (below header) showing all active sessions
- Each tab shows at-a-glance progress: current phase, topic name, and a compact status indicator (e.g., phase name + progress dots)
- Clicking a tab switches the main view to that session's full dashboard
- Each session tails its own events.jsonl (sessions may live in separate .team/ directories or use a session ID scheme)
- New sessions appear automatically when detected (file watcher or polling)
- Completed sessions remain visible (grayed or marked as shipped) until dismissed

## Design considerations

- Tab label should show enough context to distinguish sessions: topic name + current phase
- Active/running sessions should be visually distinct from completed ones
- Consider a compact progress indicator on each tab (e.g., mini phase dots or a progress bar) so you can see status without switching
- The current single-session view becomes the default when only one session exists (no tab bar needed for one session)
close_reason: Teamflow UI removed — bead no longer relevant

title: Add a dedicated shipping agent for the SHIP phase
description: The SHIP phase currently has no dedicated agent — the orchestrator handles commit + PR creation directly via /team-ship. This means there's no agent with consumes/produces in registry.json for the SHIP phase, inconsistent with the agent-per-phase pattern. Add a shipper agent that consumes verification.passed and produces feature.shipped, encapsulating the git commit, PR creation, and push logic.

title: UX reviewer must use Playwright MCP to visually inspect running application
description:
The ux-reviewer agent currently uses curl to verify HTTP responses, which cannot assess visual layout, interactive behavior, or look-and-feel. It should use the Playwright MCP tools (already available in this plugin via mcp__plugin_playwright_playwright__*) to boot the app, navigate pages, take screenshots, interact with UI elements, and visually verify expected behavior.

## Current Behavior

The ux-reviewer starts a dev server and uses curl to check:
- Response status codes
- HTML structure in response body
- Server-side error messages

This misses: visual regressions, layout issues, interactive flows (clicks, form fills, navigation), console errors, broken styles, responsiveness.

## Desired Behavior

The ux-reviewer should:
1. Start the dev server (existing behavior)
2. Use Playwright MCP tools to navigate to the application
3. Take snapshots of key pages affected by the implementation
4. Interact with UI elements (click buttons, fill forms, navigate routes)
5. Check browser console for errors/warnings
6. Verify visual layout matches expectations
7. Test interactive flows end-to-end
8. Report findings with screenshots as evidence

## Available Playwright MCP Tools

The plugin already has these registered:
- browser_navigate — load URLs
- browser_snapshot — capture accessibility tree / DOM state
- browser_click — click elements
- browser_fill_form — fill form fields
- browser_type — type text
- browser_press_key — keyboard interactions
- browser_take_screenshot — visual evidence
- browser_console_messages — check for JS errors
- browser_network_requests — verify API calls
- browser_wait_for — wait for elements/conditions
- browser_evaluate — run JS in page context
- browser_tabs — manage browser tabs
- browser_hover, browser_select_option, browser_drag — advanced interactions

## Implementation Considerations

1. Update agents/ux-reviewer.md to reference Playwright MCP tools instead of curl
2. Add the Playwright MCP tools to the agent's tools list in frontmatter (currently: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)
3. The agent should still detect project type (UI/API/Library) — Playwright is for UI projects, curl remains appropriate for API-only projects
4. Screenshots should be taken at key verification points and referenced in the report
5. The Report Format section needs updating to include visual evidence
6. Consider adding browser_install as a setup step if Playwright browsers aren't installed
7. The agent-browser and agent-browser-verify Vercel skills provide patterns for how to structure browser-based verification — review those for inspiration
notes:
gstack reference implementation: its `/qa` skill does exactly this browser-driven UX verification (over its own Playwright daemon — team-n89 rightly chooses the Playwright MCP instead, the correct call for team's no-build philosophy). Port gstack's QA *methodology*, which goes beyond the tool list already in this bead:

- Evidence checklist per page: console errors/warnings, failed network requests, broken styles, layout/responsiveness — not just status + rendered HTML.
- Find-fix-verify loop: gstack's `/qa` doesn't just report — it auto-generates a regression test for each bug found so it can't recur. Add to the Report Format: every visual/interactive bug → a Playwright regression assertion.
- Perf budget (optional / follow-up bead): gstack's `/benchmark` captures Core Web Vitals + load time + resource size as a baseline; a lightweight perf-budget check could ride along with the UX review.
- Out of scope note: gstack's `/canary` (post-deploy monitoring) is *not* for this bead — team ends at PR. Flagged only so it isn't conflated.

title: Add The Oracle agent and invocation skill to TEAM plugin
description:
Incorporate The Oracle agent into the TEAM plugin as both a standalone agent and a methodology/invocation skill. The Oracle provides deep analytical thinking, strategic problem-solving, and expert-level analysis. It should be invocable on demand during any pipeline phase when deep reasoning is needed.

## Source Content (inline — do NOT reference external dotfiles)

### Agent Definition

The Oracle is a strategic advisor powered by a strong reasoning model (opus). It provides:
- Deep code analysis and architectural insights
- Thorough code reviews with attention to correctness and edge cases
- Debugging guidance and root cause analysis
- Strategic direction for complex refactoring decisions
- Careful evaluation of trade-offs and alternatives

**Analytical Method:**
1. Understand the Goal — what is the caller trying to accomplish?
2. Identify Constraints — what must be preserved?
3. Analyze Current State — what does the code/system actually do now?
4. Evaluate Changes — what are the intended and unintended effects?
5. Consider Alternatives — are there better approaches?
6. Recommend Action — give clear, actionable guidance

**Output Format:** Summary → Analysis → Concerns → Recommendations → Alternatives

**Key Constraints:**
- Reasoning focus: prioritize deep thinking over speed
- Adhere to project standards from CLAUDE.md
- Simplification protocol: reduce state first, then coupling, then complexity, then duplication
- Comment philosophy: only explain why code ISN'T written another way
- Use Conventional Comments format for code feedback
- Intellectually humble but analytically fierce — admit uncertainty, pursue truth, show work

**Tools:** Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, WebFetch, WebSearch (investigation-capable)
**Model:** opus

### Skill Design

Create a skill (e.g. skills/oracle/SKILL.md) that:
- Registers as a slash command (/oracle) for on-demand consultation
- Accepts a question or analysis request as argument
- Dispatches The Oracle agent with the question
- Returns the structured analysis

**Trigger patterns:** 'consult the oracle', 'oracle review', 'deep analysis', 'think deeply about', 'strategic analysis', 'what should we do about'

## Integration Points

1. **Standalone use:** /oracle <question> — direct consultation outside any pipeline
2. **Pipeline use:** Other agents or the router can dispatch Oracle when they hit a decision point that needs deeper analysis (e.g., planner encountering architectural ambiguity, implementer facing a non-obvious design choice)
3. **Does NOT participate in the event loop** — Oracle is stateless and on-demand, not a pipeline phase agent with consumes/produces

## Design Decisions to Make

1. Should Oracle be a TEAM pipeline agent (with consumes/produces in registry.json) or a standalone agent that any phase can dispatch ad-hoc? Recommendation: standalone, since it doesn't fit a specific pipeline phase.
2. How does the skill interact with the agent? The skill should dispatch the agent and relay the response.
3. Overlap with existing agents: plan-critic already does adversarial plan review, systematic-debugging covers root cause analysis. Oracle is broader and deeper — it's the escalation point when standard agents need help, not a replacement.
4. The Oracle replaces the global dotfiles version at /Users/matthew/dotfiles/claude/agents/oracle.md once integrated here.

title: Incorporate clean-code-architect methodology into design and review phases
description:
Add a clean-code-architect methodology skill to the TEAM plugin, sourcing the content from the existing clean-code-architect agent definition. This methodology should be loaded by agents in the design phase (planner, implementer) and review phase (code-reviewer) to enforce clean code principles, DRY, testability, and the quality checklist.

The source content includes:
- Core philosophy (Hickey, Carmack, Armstrong, Knuth, Liskov, Ousterhout)
- Implementation standards: DRY, clean code principles, reusability, maintainability, testability
- Implementation workflow: understand requirements, design first, implement incrementally, self-review, explain decisions
- Quality checklist: single responsibility, clear naming, no magic numbers, explicit error handling, low coupling, testability, readability, DRY

Key decisions to make:
1. How much overlaps with existing solid-principles skill? Clean-code-architect covers Liskov (already in SOLID) plus broader concerns (DRY, naming, functions, testability). May need to merge or layer.
2. Which agents load it? Candidates: planner (design-first workflow), implementer (implementation standards + quality checklist), code-reviewer (quality checklist as review criteria).
3. Respect the soft limit of 3 methodology skills per agent (D7 from skill architecture research). Implementer already loads solid-principles + refactoring-to-patterns. Adding a third would hit the limit.
4. The content should be inlined into a new skills/clean-code/SKILL.md — do NOT reference the external dotfiles path.
close_reason: Shipped as engineering-standards methodology skill (renamed from clean-code)

title: Research skill architecture: thin entry points vs self-contained skills
description: Search the internet for common patterns in how plugin/skill systems handle entry point skills vs methodology skills. Specifically: should skills be thin wrappers that delegate to separate methodology files (like team-fix → test-driven-bug-fix), or should each skill be self-contained with everything inline? Look at Claude Code plugin ecosystem, other agent frameworks, and general plugin architecture patterns. The answer informs whether our current split (entry point skills + methodology skills) is the right design or if we should consolidate.
close_reason: Research completed and actionable items implemented. Wired adversarial-review to 4 review agents, documented extraction threshold, methodology load limit, and consumer info in docs/architecture.md. 7 decisions made, all shipped.

title: Integrate writing-prose methodology into technical-writer agent
description: The technical-writer agent reviews documentation gaps. Integrate the writing-prose skill methodology (clear documentation, readable explanations) so the technical-writer produces higher quality documentation assessments and recommendations.
close_reason: Closed

title: Integrate changelog updates into ship phase
description: The ship phase commits and creates PRs but does not update the changelog. Integrate the changelog skill methodology (Keep a Changelog format, user-facing changes only) so the ship phase updates CHANGELOG.md as part of shipping. Should filter out internal-only commits.
close_reason: Closed

title: Integrate SOLID principles into implementer and code-reviewer agents
description: The implementer writes code and the code-reviewer evaluates quality. Integrate the solid-principles skill (SRP, OCP, LSP, ISP, DIP) as methodology loaded by both agents — the implementer applies them while coding, the code-reviewer checks for violations.
close_reason: Closed

title: Integrate refactoring-to-patterns methodology into implementer agent
description: The implementer agent modifies existing code during implementation. Integrate Fowler's refactoring patterns methodology so the implementer recognizes code smells and applies proven transformations when working with existing code. Loaded as a methodology skill by the implementer.
close_reason: Closed

title: Integrate technical-design-doc methodology into planner agent
description: The planner agent produces implementation plans. Integrate the technical-design-doc skill methodology (architecture, trade-offs, rollout plans) so the planner can produce richer plan artifacts when the feature warrants it. The skill should be loadable by the planner agent.
close_reason: Closed

title: Integrate git-commit methodology into ship phase
description: The ship phase commits code and creates PRs. Integrate the git-commit skill methodology (50/72 rule, conventional commits, atomic commits) so the ship router produces well-formed commits. The skill should be loaded by the ship phase when creating the final commit.
close_reason: Closed

title: Enrich product-owner agent with PRD writing capability
description: The product-owner agent currently resolves ambiguity autonomously. Extend it to produce lightweight PRDs as its output artifact when feature requests are vague or complex. Integrate the product-requirements-doc methodology (structured discovery, acceptance criteria, scope boundaries). The planner agent should know how to consume a PRD artifact when one exists. This replaces the need for a separate PRD phase — the product-owner produces it as part of its existing role in the pipeline.
close_reason: Closed

title: Add bug-fix variant pipeline (/team-fix)
description: Create a compressed pipeline for bug fixes that skips research/plan and goes straight to test-driven-bug-fix discipline: reproduce → write failing test → minimal fix → verify. New entry point skill /team-fix <bug description>. Integrates the test-driven-bug-fix methodology (red-green-refactor). Events: bug.reported → tests.confirmed-failing → implementation.completed → verification.passed → ship.completed.
close_reason: Closed

title: Create evals for TEAM pipeline
description: Build an evaluation suite to measure TEAM's end-to-end quality. Evals should cover: (1) individual agent correctness — does each agent produce well-formed output for its event contract, (2) pipeline integrity — do events flow correctly through registry.json wiring, gates fire appropriately, and retry loops terminate, (3) agent quality — does the researcher find relevant files, does the planner produce actionable plans, does the security-reviewer catch known vulnerabilities, does the implementer pass tests, (4) regression detection — replay saved event logs from past features and verify outputs don't degrade. Consider using Claude Code's eval framework if available, or a custom harness that feeds synthetic feature requests through the pipeline and scores artifacts.
close_reason: Closed

title: Add Playwright to dev up dependencies
description: The TEAM plugin uses the playwright plugin for browser automation (ux-reviewer agent). Playwright browsers need to be installed locally. Add a dev.yml task to ensure Playwright is installed during dev up so contributors don't hit missing-browser errors at runtime.
close_reason: Closed

title: Add subagent worktree isolation for implementation
description: TEAM agents currently work in the main repository checkout. Add git worktree management so the implementer agent (and potentially reviewers) run in isolated worktrees. This prevents in-progress implementation from polluting the working tree and enables parallel feature work. Closes a gap vs rpikit/one-shot/superpowers which all support worktree-based isolation.
close_reason: Closed

title: Add brainstorming phase to TEAM pipeline
description: TEAM currently jumps straight to research. Add an optional brainstorming phase before research that collaboratively explores user intent, requirements, and design alternatives before committing to implementation. The superpowers brainstorming skill does this interactively — TEAM's product-owner agent only resolves ambiguity autonomously. This would close a gap vs rpikit/one-shot/superpowers plugins.
close_reason: Closed

title: Investigate autoagent for autonomous TEAM harness optimization
description: Explore using autoagent (https://github.com/kevinrgu/autoagent) to enable autonomous improvement of the TEAM pipeline. Autoagent is a framework where a meta-agent iteratively refines an agent harness by modifying prompts, tools, and routing based on benchmark scores, while humans steer direction via a program.md file. The key idea: instead of manually tuning TEAM's 12 agents, skills, and pipeline config, we could define benchmarks (e.g. feature implementation quality, pipeline completion rate, review accuracy) and let a meta-agent optimize prompts, model tiering, and orchestration automatically. Areas to investigate: (1) adapting autoagent's program.md/agent.py pattern to TEAM's distributed plugin architecture, (2) defining meaningful benchmarks for pipeline quality, (3) whether Docker isolation or worktree isolation fits better, (4) which TEAM components benefit most from automated optimization (agent prompts, skill content, registry config).

title: Add empty state screen to Teamflow dashboard
description:
When the Teamflow server starts with no active pipeline (.team/events.jsonl missing or empty), the dashboard shows idle phase cards and a blank timeline. There is no explicit message telling the user what to do next.

## Current behavior

- Header shows no topic, no duration
- PhaseCards render all 12 agents as idle
- Timeline is empty
- No guidance for the user

## Expected behavior

Show a clear empty state message like 'Waiting for pipeline... Run /team to start' with brief instructions. Replace the phase cards and timeline with the empty state until the first feature.requested event arrives.

## Context

Discovered during the dev server integration work (team-jdr). The server now runs independently via dev server, making the empty state more visible since users start the server before any pipeline runs.
close_reason: Closed

title: Add motion and animation to TeamFlow visualization UI
description:
The TeamFlow visualization UI (the pipeline progress display shown during /team runs) is currently static. Add tasteful motion and animation to make the pipeline feel alive and communicate state changes clearly.

## Potential Animations

- Phase transitions: smooth slide/fade when moving between pipeline phases
- Agent dispatch: subtle pulse or glow when an agent is dispatched
- Parallel agents: staggered entrance animations when multiple agents launch simultaneously
- Completion: check mark or success animation when an agent returns
- Gate evaluation: visual indicator while gates are being evaluated (spinning, pulsing)
- Event flow: animated connection lines between events in the pipeline visualization
- Progress: smooth progress bar or step indicator transitions
- Error/failure: shake or red flash when a gate fails or agent errors

## Design Principles

- Motion should communicate state, not decorate — every animation should convey information
- Keep animations fast (150-300ms) to feel responsive, not sluggish
- Respect reduced-motion preferences (prefers-reduced-motion media query)
- Use easing curves that feel natural (ease-out for entrances, ease-in for exits)
- Don't block interaction — animations should be non-blocking
close_reason: Teamflow UI removed — bead no longer relevant

title: Add retrospective phase for agent self-improvement
description: After a feature ships (/team-ship), add an optional retrospective phase where each agent that participated in the pipeline reflects on what could have been done better. Each agent reviews the event log and artifacts from its phase, identifies friction points or mistakes, and proposes improvements to its own system prompt or methodology skill. This enables continuous self-improvement of the agent mesh over time. Design considerations: new event (e.g. ship.completed → retrospective.requested), a retrospective agent or reuse of each original agent in review mode, structured output format for improvement proposals, and a mechanism to apply approved changes back to agent/skill definitions.

title: Switch dev update back to npx skills update
description: npx skills update ignores project-level skills-lock.json (vercel-labs/skills#775, #690). Currently using a workaround that reinstalls from source. Switch back once the upstream bug is fixed.
